title: Feature Engineering
description: Feature selection, encoding, scaling, leakage, missing data, time features, and embedding features.

questions.title: Why is feature engineering important?
questions.scenario: A tabular model performs poorly even though the algorithm is strong.
questions.expectedAnswer: Feature engineering turns raw data into useful model inputs. Good features can improve accuracy, stability, interpretability, and data efficiency. Candidates should mention domain knowledge, leakage prevention, feature freshness, and production availability.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Feature Engineering?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise feature engineering setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: How do you encode categorical variables?
questions.scenario: A fraud model has merchant IDs, country codes, and device types.
questions.expectedAnswer: Options include one-hot encoding, ordinal encoding, target encoding, hashing, learned embeddings, and frequency features. The choice depends on cardinality, model type, leakage risk, interpretability, and unseen categories. Target encoding must be done with cross-validation or time-aware methods to avoid leakage.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Feature Engineering?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise feature engineering setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: How do you handle missing data?
questions.scenario: Important customer attributes are missing for newer users.
questions.expectedAnswer: Approaches include deletion, mean or median imputation, model-based imputation, missing indicators, domain-specific defaults, or treating missing as a category. The right approach depends on why data is missing and whether missingness itself is predictive.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Feature Engineering?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise feature engineering setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: How can feature engineering introduce leakage?
questions.scenario: A feature uses total refunds in a period that includes time after the prediction event.
questions.expectedAnswer: Leakage appears when features use future information, target-derived signals, improper aggregations, or preprocessing fitted on full data. Time-aware splits, pipeline isolation, feature lineage, and production simulation help prevent it.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Feature Engineering?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise feature engineering setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: When would you use embeddings as ML features?
questions.scenario: A churn model wants to include support ticket text and product descriptions.
questions.expectedAnswer: Embeddings are useful for text, images, products, users, or entities where semantic similarity matters. They can improve classical models but add dependency on embedding models, versioning, drift monitoring, and explainability challenges.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.

questions.title: How would you explain feature selection in a Feature Engineering interview?
questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through feature selection for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define feature selection, explain why it matters for feature engineering, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations.
